exercises.title: Match the abstraction to the problem shape
exercises.prompt: The lesson's thesis: don't pick the 'best' framework, match the framework's core abstraction to your problem's shape. State the core abstraction of LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, and Agno, and the problem shape each fits.
exercises.hint: LangGraph: state graph (branching/durable workflows). CrewAI: roles/crew (role-division tasks). AutoGen: conversation between agents (chat-style collaboration). Agno: single-agent (one capable agent, fast).
exercises.solution:
**The thesis:** every framework sells the same demo (a research agent builds a report) and hides the same bug (its abstractions leak when your problem doesn't match its shape). The fix isn't 'find the best framework' — it's to **match the framework's *core abstraction* to the *shape* of your problem**. Everything that doesn't fit becomes 'glue you write twice.'

**The four frameworks' core abstractions and the problem shape each fits:**
- **LangGraph — a state graph.** Core abstraction: an **explicit state machine** (nodes, edges, typed state, checkpoints, interrupts). **Fits:** workflows shaped like **state machines** — branching/conditional control flow, cycles, **durable long-running** processes, and **human-in-the-loop** approval gates (Lesson 16). Best when you need persistence, interrupts, and complex routing.
- **CrewAI — roles / a crew.** Core abstraction: **role-based agents** ('researcher', 'writer', 'reviewer') collaborating on a task. **Fits:** problems that **decompose cleanly into roles** with a natural division of labor (a content pipeline: research → write → edit). Best when the work maps to distinct specialist roles.
- **AutoGen — conversation between agents.** Core abstraction: **multi-agent chat** — agents (and humans) talk to each other to solve a problem. **Fits:** **collaborative, conversational** problem-solving where agents iterate via dialogue (e.g. a coder agent and a critic agent discussing), and flexible, emergent interaction patterns.
- **Agno — a single capable agent.** Core abstraction: **one well-equipped agent** (tools, memory) optimized for **simplicity and speed**. **Fits:** **single-agent** tasks where you want a fast, lean assistant without multi-agent orchestration overhead.

**The point:** these are **different bets**, not better/worse. LangGraph bets your problem is a **state machine**; CrewAI bets it's a **role division**; AutoGen bets it's a **conversation**; Agno bets it's a **single agent**. Pick the one whose **core abstraction matches your problem's natural shape** — and the framework helps you; pick wrong and you fight it.

exercises.title: Where abstractions leak
exercises.prompt: Each framework's abstraction leaks when forced outside its sweet spot. Explain the concrete leak for each (CrewAI role handoff, AutoGen no first-class state, LangGraph naming transitions upfront, Agno fan-out), and what 'the state schema fights the orchestration layer' means.
exercises.hint: CrewAI: passing a structured plan researcher→writer is awkward. AutoGen: state is just a pickled chat log, no real state. LangGraph: must name every transition before you know the agent's behavior. Agno: single-agent abstraction struggles with concurrent workers. The leak = your data model and the framework's control model disagree.
exercises.solution:
**The concrete leaks (the lesson's examples):**
- **CrewAI — role handoff:** roles work until the **'researcher' must hand a structured plan to the 'writer'**. CrewAI gives you roles but **fights you on passing structured data between them** — the handoff is conversational/loose, so transferring a typed plan (not just a text blob) is awkward and lossy. The abstraction handles *who does what* but not *structured data flow between roles*.
- **AutoGen — no first-class state:** AutoGen gives **agent-to-agent chat**, but **has no first-class state** — your 'state' is effectively a **pickled conversation log**. So **checkpointing, structured state, and durability** are bolted on / fragile; you can't cleanly inspect or persist a typed state, only a transcript. Great for conversation, weak for stateful workflows.
- **LangGraph — naming transitions upfront:** LangGraph's explicit graph forces you to **name every node and transition *before* you know how the agent will actually behave** (Lesson 16). For **exploratory or open-ended** agents whose control flow you're still discovering, declaring the topology upfront is friction — you're committing to structure prematurely.
- **Agno — fan-out:** Agno's **single-agent** abstraction is lean and fast, but **screams when you try to fan out to multiple concurrent workers** — it wasn't built for parallel multi-agent orchestration, so forcing concurrency fights the abstraction.

**What 'the state schema fights the orchestration layer' means:** every agent system has two things: a **data model** (what information flows — the plan, the intermediate results, the task state) and a **control/orchestration model** (how execution moves — who runs next, handoffs, branching, concurrency). A leak happens when **your problem's natural data model disagrees with the framework's control model**:
- You need to pass a **structured plan** (data model) but the framework's orchestration only supports **conversational handoffs** (CrewAI) → you serialize/deserialize and lose structure.
- You need **durable typed state** (data model) but the framework only persists a **chat log** (AutoGen) → your checkpoint is a fragile pickle.
- You need **flexible/emergent control flow** but the framework demands **predeclared transitions** (LangGraph) → you over-specify.
- You need **concurrent workers** but the framework assumes **one agent** (Agno) → you fight to parallelize.
In each case, the **shape of the data you need to move** and the **shape of the execution the framework imposes** are **mismatched**, so you end up writing **glue code to bridge them** — translating between your data model and the framework's control model at every step. That glue is the 'leak': the framework stops helping and starts being something you work *around*. The diagnostic: if you're constantly **serializing structured data to fit a loose handoff**, or **bolting state onto a stateless framework**, or **predeclaring flow you don't know yet**, your problem's shape doesn't match the framework's abstraction.

exercises.title: Choosing a framework (or none) and the build-vs-adopt decision
exercises.prompt: Give a decision framework for choosing an agent framework (or writing your own loop). Cover how to diagnose your problem's shape, the cost of framework lock-in and leaky abstractions, when 'no framework' (plain function-calling loop) wins, and how MCP and standards reduce the stakes of the choice. Why is 'glue you write twice' the real cost?
exercises.hint: Diagnose: is it a state machine, role division, conversation, or single agent? Match abstraction. Cost of wrong pick = rewriting glue + fighting the framework. No-framework wins for simple/linear/well-understood agents. MCP standardizes tools so you're not locked into one framework's tool format. Glue twice = you pay the integration cost the framework was supposed to save.
exercises.solution:
**Decision framework — diagnose the problem shape first:**
1. **Characterize your problem's shape:**
   - **Branching/durable/human-gated workflow** (conditional control flow, long-running, approval steps) → **state machine** → **LangGraph**.
   - **Clean role division** (distinct specialists, pipeline) → **roles** → **CrewAI**.
   - **Conversational/collaborative** problem-solving (agents iterating via dialogue) → **agent chat** → **AutoGen**.
   - **Single capable agent**, simplicity/speed priority → **single-agent** → **Agno**.
   - **Simple, linear, or well-understood** tool-use loop → **no framework** (plain function-calling loop, Lesson 9).
2. **Check where it would leak:** for each candidate, ask 'does my **data model** (what I need to pass around) fit its **control model** (how it orchestrates)?' (previous exercise). If you'd be writing glue to bridge them, it's the wrong fit.
3. **Match, don't maximize:** pick the framework whose **core abstraction matches your shape** — not the most popular or feature-rich one.

**The cost of framework lock-in and leaky abstractions:** adopting a framework is a **commitment** — your code is structured around its abstractions (state, roles, chat, nodes), its conventions, and its tool format. If the abstraction **leaks** (doesn't match your problem), you pay twice: **(1)** you **fight the framework** (workarounds, glue, serializing data to fit its model — the leaks above), and **(2)** if you later **switch frameworks**, you **rewrite all that orchestration and glue**. Framework churn is expensive because the framework touches your control flow everywhere. A wrong pick isn't just suboptimal — it's a tax on every feature and a costly migration later.

**When 'no framework' (plain function-calling loop) wins:** for **simple, short, linear, or well-understood** agents — a few tool calls, no complex branching, no durability/human-gate/multi-agent needs — a **hand-written function-calling loop (Lesson 9)** is **simpler, more transparent, easier to debug, and has zero lock-in**. You own the control flow with no abstraction tax. Many production 'agents' are genuinely this simple, and a framework would add complexity for capabilities you don't use (Lesson 16's 'when LangGraph is overkill'). **Don't adopt a framework to solve a problem a loop solves cleanly** — frameworks earn their keep only when you need what they provide (durability, roles, multi-agent, complex flow).

**How MCP and standards reduce the stakes:** the **biggest lock-in cost is usually the tools/integrations** — historically each framework had its own tool format, so switching meant rewriting tool definitions (the N×M problem, Lesson 14). **MCP standardizes the tool/data interface**: you expose tools via **one MCP server**, and **any framework (or no framework) that speaks MCP can use them**. This **decouples your tools from your orchestration framework** — you can switch LangGraph ↔ CrewAI ↔ a plain loop **without rewriting your tools**, because they live behind MCP. Standards like MCP shrink the framework choice from a deep architectural commitment to a more **swappable orchestration layer**, lowering the cost of picking wrong (and of changing your mind).

**Why 'glue you write twice' is the real cost:** frameworks promise to **save you integration/orchestration work**. But when the abstraction **doesn't fit your problem**, you end up **writing the glue the framework was supposed to provide** — translating your data model to its control model, bolting on missing state, bridging handoffs — and then, if you ever switch (or the framework's abstraction keeps fighting you), you **write that glue *again*** for the next framework. So you've paid the integration cost the framework was meant to eliminate, **twice** (once to fit the framework, once to escape it), plus the framework's own complexity. That's the lesson's punchline: the cost of a mismatched framework isn't the framework — it's the **duplicated glue** at the seam between your problem's shape and the framework's abstraction. **Pick the abstraction that matches your shape (or none), keep tools behind MCP so the orchestration layer stays swappable, and you minimize the glue** — which is the whole game in choosing agent infrastructure.
